back: The management of the activities that create goods and services. It focuses on turning inputs like labor, materials, equipment, and information into outputs customers value.
Physical products that customers can see, touch, store, and transport. Examples include cars, phones, food, and furniture.
Activities or experiences provided to customers rather than physical products. Examples include banking, healthcare, consulting, transportation, and education.
The process of converting inputs into outputs. In business, this means using resources to create goods or services customers want.
The resources used to produce a good or service. Common inputs include labor, materials, machines, money, information, and time.
The goods or services produced by an operation. Outputs are what the customer receives or uses.
Work that increases the usefulness or worth of a product or service from the customer's point of view. Managers try to increase value-added work and reduce waste.
A measure of how efficiently inputs are turned into outputs. Productivity = Output ÷ Input.
Productivity measured using only one type of input, such as labor hours or machine hours. Single-Factor Productivity = Output ÷ One Input.
Productivity measured using more than one input, such as labor, materials, and overhead together. Multifactor Productivity = Output ÷ Combined Inputs.
How well resources are used with minimal waste. Efficiency often compares actual output to expected or standard output.
How well an operation achieves the right goals or customer outcomes. A process can be efficient but not effective if it does the wrong thing quickly.
The plan for how operations will support the company's overall business strategy. It decides what the operation should be especially good at, such as low cost, speed, quality, or flexibility.
The performance areas a company uses to compete in the market. Common priorities include cost, quality, speed, flexibility, and reliability.
The money spent to produce and deliver a good or service. Competing on cost means designing operations to keep expenses low while still meeting customer needs.
How well a product or service meets customer expectations. Quality can mean durability, accuracy, appearance, performance, or consistency.
How quickly a company can produce, deliver, or respond to customers. Faster operations can reduce waiting time and improve customer satisfaction.
The ability of an operation to change what it makes, how much it makes, or how quickly it adapts. Flexible operations can respond better to changing customer needs.
The ability to deliver what was promised dependably and consistently. In operations, reliability often means meeting due dates, specifications, and service commitments.
A factor that causes customers to choose one company's product or service over competitors. For example, the lowest price or fastest delivery may be the order winner.
A minimum requirement a company must meet just to be considered by customers. Order qualifiers do not usually win the sale, but failing them can eliminate the company from consideration.
A production approach where goods are made before customers place specific orders. It works best when demand is predictable and customers expect fast availability.
A production approach where work begins only after a customer places an order. It allows customization but usually creates longer wait times.
A production approach where standard parts are made ahead of time and assembled after a customer order arrives. It balances speed with some customization.
The ability to produce customized products or services at a scale and cost closer to mass production. It often relies on modular design, flexible processes, and strong information systems.
An operations approach that considers people, planet, and profit. Managers evaluate performance using social, environmental, and financial results.
A set of steps that turns inputs into outputs. In operations, processes show how work actually gets done.
A visual picture of the steps, decisions, and flows in a process. It helps managers see delays, handoffs, bottlenecks, and waste.
A diagram that shows the sequence of steps in a process using symbols and arrows. It is commonly used to analyze how work moves from start to finish.
One activity or task within a larger process. Each step should be examined to see whether it adds value, creates delay, or causes errors.
A place in a process where the next step depends on a condition or choice. Decision points often appear as yes-or-no branches in a flowchart.
The transfer of work, information, or responsibility from one person, team, or system to another. Handoffs can create delays, confusion, or errors if not managed well.
The time between completing one unit and completing the next unit. Cycle Time = Available Production Time ÷ Units Produced.
The total time it takes for one unit to move through the entire process from start to finish. It includes processing time, waiting time, movement, inspection, and delays.
The number of units a process completes in a given period of time. Throughput Rate = Units Completed ÷ Time.
The maximum output a process, machine, worker, or facility can produce over a period of time. Capacity helps managers know whether demand can be met.
The maximum output a system could produce under ideal conditions. It is usually higher than what the operation can realistically produce every day.
The realistic maximum output after considering normal limits like maintenance, staffing, breaks, product mix, and setup time. It is usually lower than design capacity.
How much of design capacity is actually being used. Utilization = Actual Output ÷ Design Capacity × 100.
How much of effective capacity is actually being achieved. Efficiency = Actual Output ÷ Effective Capacity × 100.
The slowest step or resource in a process that limits the output of the whole system. Improving non-bottleneck steps may not increase total output.
Anything that limits a system's ability to achieve more output or better performance. Constraints can be machines, labor, materials, policies, space, or demand.
A management approach focused on finding and improving the system's main constraint. The basic idea is that the whole process improves most when the bottleneck improves.
The effort to make a process faster, cheaper, safer, more reliable, or higher quality. It usually starts by measuring current performance and identifying waste or problems.
An activity that directly improves the product or service in a way the customer cares about. Managers try to protect and improve these activities.
An activity that uses time or resources but does not increase customer value. Examples include unnecessary waiting, movement, rework, and duplicate approvals.
A major redesign of a process rather than a small improvement. It asks what the process should look like if it were built from scratch.
A process type that produces low-volume, highly customized work. Examples include repair shops, custom furniture makers, and specialized medical clinics.
A process type that produces groups of similar items together. It offers more variety than repetitive production but usually has more setup time and waiting.
A process type that produces standardized products in high volume using a repeated sequence of steps. Assembly lines are a common example.
A process type that produces a very high volume of a standardized product with little interruption. Examples include oil refining, paper production, and chemical processing.
A process that delivers an activity, experience, or solution to a customer. Service processes often involve customer contact, waiting time, and variation in customer needs.

A framework that matches product variety and volume with the best process type. Low-volume custom products usually fit job shops, while high-volume standardized products fit repetitive or continuous processes.
A calculation used to find the volume where total revenue equals total cost. Break-Even Quantity = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price per Unit − Variable Cost per Unit).
The degree to which a product or service meets customer needs and expectations. In operations, quality means both doing the right things and doing them consistently.
How closely a product or service matches its design, specifications, or standards. A product with high conformance quality is made consistently with few defects.
How well a product or service performs in the eyes of the customer. It includes features, durability, service level, and overall usefulness.
A company-wide approach to continuously improving quality in every process and department. TQM emphasizes customer focus, employee involvement, prevention, and continuous improvement.
The process of checking finished work or work in progress to detect defects. It focuses on finding problems before products or services reach customers.
The process of designing systems to prevent defects from happening in the first place. It focuses on building quality into the process rather than inspecting it afterward.
Costs spent to avoid defects before they happen. Examples include training, process design, supplier certification, and preventive maintenance.
Costs spent to inspect, test, or measure quality. Examples include product inspections, audits, testing equipment, and quality reviews.
Costs from defects found before the product or service reaches the customer. Examples include scrap, rework, downtime, and retesting.
Costs from defects found after the product or service reaches the customer. Examples include returns, warranties, complaints, lawsuits, and lost reputation.
The total cost of preventing, detecting, and fixing quality problems. Cost of Quality = Prevention Costs + Appraisal Costs + Internal Failure Costs + External Failure Costs.
A data-driven method for reducing defects and process variation. Its goal is to make processes highly consistent and close to customer requirements.
A five-step Six Sigma improvement process: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control. It is used to solve process problems with data.
A measure of how many defects occur for every one million chances for defects. DPMO = (Number of Defects ÷ Number of Opportunities) × 1,000,000.
The use of data and charts to monitor whether a process is staying stable over time. It helps managers detect unusual changes before they become bigger problems.
A graph used to track process performance over time with a center line and control limits. It helps show whether variation looks normal or unusual.
The highest expected value for a stable process on a control chart. Points above this limit may signal a special problem or unusual cause.
The lowest expected value for a stable process on a control chart. Points below this limit may signal a special problem or unusual cause.
Natural variation that is built into a stable process. It is expected unless the process itself is changed.
Unusual variation caused by a specific event or problem outside the normal process. Examples include machine failure, bad materials, or an untrained worker.
A measure of whether a process can consistently produce output within customer or design limits. A capable process produces results that fit within the required range.

A measure of potential process capability assuming the process is centered between the specification limits. Cp = (Upper Specification Limit − Lower Specification Limit) ÷ (6 × Process Standard Deviation).
A measure of process capability that accounts for whether the process is centered. Cpk = Smaller of [(USL − Mean) ÷ (3σ), (Mean − LSL) ÷ (3σ)].
A set of international quality management standards. Certification signals that a company has documented processes for consistently meeting customer and regulatory requirements.
A U.S. award that recognizes organizations with excellent management and quality performance. It is often used as a model for evaluating overall organizational excellence.
A philosophy of continuous, small improvements involving employees at all levels. The goal is to keep improving processes instead of waiting for major redesigns.
A mistake-proofing method that prevents errors or makes them immediately obvious. Examples include connectors that only fit one way or forms that require missing fields to be completed.
The goal of doing work right the first time with no errors. In practice, it pushes managers to prevent defects rather than accept them as normal.
The needs, expectations, complaints, and preferences customers express or reveal. Operations teams use it to design products, services, and processes that actually fit customer priorities.
A planning method that translates customer needs into specific product or process features. It helps connect what customers want with what the company must design and control.
Goods or materials a company holds for future use or sale. Inventory can help meet demand, but it also ties up cash and creates storage risk.
The portion of inventory used to meet normal demand between orders. It exists because companies usually order or produce in batches rather than one unit at a time.
Extra inventory held to protect against uncertainty in demand or lead time. More safety stock reduces stockout risk but increases holding cost.
Inventory that has been ordered or produced but is still in transit or not yet available for use. It exists because moving goods through a supply chain takes time.
Demand for a finished product or item that comes directly from customers or the market. It must usually be forecasted because it is not calculated from another item's demand.
Demand for an item that depends on the demand for another item. For example, demand for tires depends on how many cars are being produced.
The order size that minimizes total annual ordering and holding costs. EOQ = √[(2DS) ÷ H], where D = annual demand, S = ordering cost per order, and H = annual holding cost per unit.
The cost of keeping inventory over time. It can include storage, insurance, spoilage, theft, damage, and the opportunity cost of cash tied up in inventory.
The cost of placing and receiving an inventory order. It can include purchasing paperwork, supplier communication, shipping setup, receiving, and inspection.
The cost of not having inventory available when it is needed. It can include lost sales, delays, unhappy customers, emergency orders, and damaged reputation.
The inventory level that triggers a new order. Reorder Point = Demand During Lead Time + Safety Stock.
The time between placing an order and receiving it. Longer or less predictable lead times usually require more inventory protection.
The amount of inventory expected to be used while waiting for a new order to arrive. Demand During Lead Time = Average Demand per Period × Lead Time.
The probability of having enough inventory to meet demand without a stockout during a replenishment cycle. Higher service levels usually require more safety stock.
The percentage of customer demand that is immediately filled from available inventory. Fill Rate = Demand Filled Immediately ÷ Total Demand × 100.
A measure of how many times inventory is sold or used during a period. Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Inventory.
A measure of how many days inventory will last on average. Days of Inventory = 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover.
An inventory method that groups items by importance, usually based on annual dollar usage. Managers give the most attention to A items and less attention to lower-value C items.
The most important inventory items, often small in number but high in dollar value or business impact. These items need tight control, accurate records, and frequent review.
Inventory items with moderate value or importance. They receive more control than C items but less intensive attention than A items.
Inventory items with low value or low business impact. They are often controlled with simpler systems because close management may cost more than it saves.
An inventory approach that aims to receive materials only when they are needed for production or sale. It reduces inventory costs but requires reliable suppliers and stable processes.
A visual signal used to trigger production or replenishment. Kanban helps control inventory by pulling work forward only when it is needed.
An arrangement where the supplier monitors and replenishes the customer's inventory. It can reduce stockouts and improve coordination between buyer and supplier.
Inventory stored at the customer's location but still owned by the supplier until it is used or sold. This can reduce the buyer's inventory investment while keeping goods available.
An inventory system where stock levels are checked at fixed time intervals. Orders are placed after each review to bring inventory back up to a target level.
An inventory system where stock levels are monitored constantly or after every transaction. A new order is placed when inventory reaches the reorder point.
A simple inventory system that uses two containers of stock. When the first bin is empty, a reorder is triggered while the second bin covers demand during lead time.
An inventory system with a minimum level that triggers an order and a maximum level used as the target restock amount. It helps prevent both stockouts and excessive inventory.
The network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources that move a product or service from raw materials to the final customer.
The coordination of sourcing, production, logistics, and information flow so the right products reach customers at the right cost, quality, and speed.
The part of the supply chain closer to suppliers and raw materials. Managers look upstream when they need to improve sourcing, supplier quality, or input costs.
The part of the supply chain closer to distributors, retailers, and final customers. Managers look downstream when they need to improve delivery, service, or demand information.
A supplier that sells directly to the company. For example, a seat manufacturer that sells directly to an automaker is a tier 1 supplier.
A supplier that sells to a tier 1 supplier rather than directly to the main company. Problems at tier 2 suppliers can still disrupt the final product.
A company that makes the final branded product or major equipment using parts from suppliers. In many supply chains, the OEM coordinates many supplier relationships.
The degree to which a company coordinates decisions, systems, and information with suppliers and customers. Higher integration can reduce delays, errors, and excess inventory.
Owning or controlling more stages of the supply chain, such as a manufacturer buying a supplier or distributor. It can improve control but may increase cost and reduce flexibility.
Having another company perform work that could be done internally. Managers use outsourcing to lower costs, access expertise, or focus on core activities.
Moving business activities to another country, often to reduce costs or access global capabilities. It can create savings but may add risks from distance, politics, and logistics.
Moving production or sourcing closer to the home market but still in a nearby country. Companies use nearshoring to reduce lead times, transportation risk, and coordination problems.
Bringing production or sourcing back to the company's home country. Managers consider reshoring when speed, control, risk reduction, or quality matter more than low labor cost.
A decision about whether to produce something internally or purchase it from an outside supplier. Managers compare cost, quality, capacity, control, risk, and strategic importance.
The process of choosing suppliers based on factors like price, quality, reliability, capacity, technology, location, and risk. Good supplier selection affects cost and customer service.
The process of measuring supplier performance over time. Common measures include delivery reliability, defect rates, cost, responsiveness, and financial stability.
Helping suppliers improve their performance through training, process improvement, technology support, or joint problem solving. It is used when a supplier is important but needs improvement.
A close, long-term relationship between companies that work together for mutual benefit. Partnerships often involve trust, shared information, joint improvement, and reduced transaction friction.
The ability to see what is happening across the supply chain, such as inventory levels, shipment status, supplier capacity, and demand changes. Better visibility helps managers react faster.
The exchange of data between supply chain partners, such as forecasts, orders, inventory, and production schedules. Sharing information helps reduce uncertainty and poor decisions.
A supply chain problem where small changes in customer demand become larger order swings upstream. It often happens because of poor information sharing, batching, promotions, and panic ordering.
The growth of demand variability as orders move upstream in a supply chain. It causes suppliers to overreact, creating excess inventory or shortages.
A supply chain approach where partners jointly plan promotions, forecasts, and replenishment. The goal is to align demand expectations and reduce stockouts or excess inventory.
An unexpected event that interrupts the normal flow of goods, services, money, or information. Examples include supplier failures, natural disasters, port delays, strikes, and cyberattacks.
A supply chain's ability to prepare for, respond to, and recover from disruptions. Resilient systems may use backup suppliers, flexible capacity, inventory buffers, or faster information flow.
Extra capacity, suppliers, inventory, or resources kept as backup in case something fails. It can reduce risk but often increases cost.
The process of identifying, assessing, and reducing risks that could disrupt the supply chain. Managers balance low cost with reliability and resilience.

Managing supply chain decisions to reduce environmental harm and support ethical, long-term business practices. It can include lower emissions, responsible sourcing, waste reduction, and labor standards.
The movement of products backward from customers to companies for returns, repairs, recycling, reuse, or disposal. It matters for customer service, cost control, and sustainability.
A supply chain that includes both forward product flow to customers and reverse flow for returns, reuse, recycling, or remanufacturing. The goal is to recover value instead of wasting materials.
A supply chain approach that designs products and processes so materials stay in use longer through reuse, repair, recycling, or remanufacturing. It reduces waste and dependence on new raw materials.
An estimate of future demand, sales, costs, or other business activity. Forecasts are never perfect, but they help managers plan capacity, inventory, staffing, and budgets.
An estimate of how much customers will want in a future period. It is used to plan production, staffing, purchasing, and inventory.
Forecasting based on judgment, expert opinion, market research, or customer input instead of only past data. It is useful when historical data is limited or the situation is new.
Forecasting based on numerical data and mathematical methods. It works best when past patterns are likely to continue into the future.
A set of data points recorded over time, such as monthly sales or weekly demand. Time series forecasting looks for patterns in past demand to estimate future demand.
A long-term upward or downward movement in data over time. For example, sales growing steadily each month shows an upward trend.
A repeating pattern in demand tied to time periods like days, weeks, months, or seasons. For example, winter coats usually have seasonal demand.
Longer-term demand movement caused by economic or industry cycles. Unlike seasonality, cycles are less regular and may last multiple years.
Unpredictable movement in demand caused by chance events or noise. Managers cannot fully forecast random variation, so they often use buffers like safety stock.
A simple forecast that uses the most recent actual demand as the next period's forecast. Formula: Forecast for next period = Actual demand this period.
A forecast that averages demand from the most recent set number of periods. Formula: Moving Average = Sum of demand for n periods ÷ n.
A forecast that averages recent demand while giving different importance to each period. Formula: Weighted Moving Average = Sum of each demand × its weight.
A forecasting method that updates the old forecast using the latest error. Formula: New Forecast = Old Forecast + α(Actual Demand − Old Forecast).
Alpha, written as α, controls how much weight exponential smoothing gives to the newest demand data. A higher α reacts faster, while a lower α creates a smoother forecast.
A forecasting method that uses exponential smoothing but also adjusts for an upward or downward trend. It is useful when demand is not level over time.
A measure of forecast error that averages the absolute size of the errors. Formula: MAD = Sum of |Actual − Forecast| ÷ n.
A measure of forecast error that averages squared errors, which heavily penalizes large mistakes. Formula: MSE = Sum of (Actual − Forecast)² ÷ n.
A forecast accuracy measure that shows average error as a percentage of actual demand. Formula: MAPE = Sum of |Actual − Forecast| ÷ Actual × 100 ÷ n.
A measure used to see whether forecasts are consistently too high or too low. Formula: Tracking Signal = Cumulative Forecast Error ÷ MAD.
A consistent tendency for forecasts to be too high or too low. Bias matters because repeated small errors can create major inventory, staffing, or capacity problems.
The difference between actual demand and forecasted demand. Formula: Forecast Error = Actual Demand − Forecast Demand.
Forecasting that uses input from multiple groups or supply chain partners, such as sales, operations, suppliers, and customers. It improves forecasts by combining different information sources.
A cross-functional planning process that aligns demand forecasts with production, inventory, workforce, and financial plans. It helps the company agree on one realistic operating plan.
Actions a company takes to influence or match demand, such as pricing, promotions, reservations, backorders, or shifting customers to different times. It helps balance demand with available capacity.
Forecasting demand for a product with little or no sales history. Managers often use market research, similar products, expert judgment, test markets, and early sales data.
An operations approach focused on creating customer value while removing waste. Lean managers improve flow, reduce delays, and solve problems at the source.
Any activity that uses resources but does not add value from the customer's point of view. Lean systems try to find and remove waste continuously.

The seven common types of waste in lean are overproduction, waiting, transportation, over-processing, excess inventory, unnecessary motion, and defects. They help managers spot improvement opportunities.
Making more than is needed or making it earlier than needed. Lean treats overproduction as especially harmful because it creates extra inventory and hides problems.
Idle time when people, machines, materials, or information are not ready when needed. Waiting increases lead time without adding customer value.
Unnecessary movement of materials, products, or information between places. It adds cost and delay without directly improving the product.
Doing more work, adding more features, or using more precision than the customer needs. It wastes time and resources without increasing customer value.
Holding more materials, work in process, or finished goods than needed. It ties up cash, takes space, and can hide quality or process problems.
Extra movement by workers, such as reaching, walking, searching, or bending more than needed. It wastes time and can increase fatigue or injury risk.
Errors, rework, scrap, or failures that require correction or replacement. Defects waste labor, materials, time, and customer trust.
A visual tool that maps the steps, information flows, delays, and inventory needed to deliver a product or service. It helps managers see waste and redesign the process.
A value stream map showing how a process works today. It is used to identify delays, bottlenecks, inventory buildup, and waste.
A value stream map showing the improved process the organization wants to create. It becomes a practical roadmap for lean improvement.
The pace of production needed to match customer demand. Formula: Takt Time = Available Production Time ÷ Customer Demand.
Moving one item at a time through process steps with little or no waiting between them. It reduces inventory, exposes problems quickly, and shortens lead time.
A system where work is triggered by actual customer demand or the next process's need. Pull systems reduce overproduction and excess inventory.
A system where work is produced based on a schedule or forecast and pushed to the next step. Push systems can be useful but often create excess inventory if forecasts are wrong.
A visual signal system that controls when to produce or move materials in a pull system. A kanban card, bin, or signal tells the previous step to replenish only what is needed.
A lean workplace organization method built around sort, set in order, shine, standardize, and sustain. The goal is to make work easier, safer, and more reliable.
The first 5S step: remove unnecessary items from the work area. It reduces clutter and makes needed tools easier to find.
The 5S step of arranging needed items so they are easy to find, use, and return. A place for everything reduces searching and motion waste.
The 5S step of cleaning the workplace and equipment regularly. Cleaning helps reveal problems like leaks, damage, or unsafe conditions.
The 5S step of creating consistent routines and visual standards. Standardization helps make good practices repeatable.
The 5S step of maintaining the system over time through habits, audits, and accountability. Without sustain, improvements tend to fade.
Using visible signals, labels, boards, colors, or displays so people can quickly understand process status. It makes problems easier to see and act on.
A signal workers use to stop production or call for help when they see a problem. It supports fast problem solving instead of letting defects continue.
A lean principle meaning quality is built into the process, often by stopping work when a problem occurs. It prevents defects from moving downstream.
A lean method of smoothing production volume and mix over time. It reduces uneven workloads, inventory swings, and stress on the system.
Spreading production evenly instead of reacting sharply to every demand change. It helps stabilize schedules, labor, suppliers, and equipment use.
A lean method for reducing equipment setup or changeover time. Shorter changeovers make smaller batches and more flexible production possible.
A maintenance approach where operators and maintenance teams work together to keep equipment reliable. The goal is fewer breakdowns, less downtime, and better productivity.
A measure of how effectively equipment is being used. Formula: OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality.
Applying lean ideas to service work by reducing delays, errors, handoffs, rework, and unnecessary steps. The goal is faster, more reliable service for customers.
Using lean tools in healthcare to reduce patient waiting, errors, unnecessary movement, supply waste, and process delays. The goal is better patient care with less wasted effort.
The process of deciding how much productive ability an operation needs to meet demand. It affects facilities, labor, equipment, suppliers, and customer service.
Planning for capacity and resources over a longer time horizon, often years. It includes major decisions like facilities, equipment, technology, and market expansion.
Planning over a medium time horizon, often several months to about a year. It usually focuses on workforce levels, inventory, subcontracting, and production rates.
Planning over days, weeks, or a few months. It focuses on detailed schedules, job assignments, staffing, machine use, and order timing.
The process of checking whether enough labor, machines, and work-center capacity exist to meet a production plan. It helps managers find overloads before they become delays.
Medium-term planning that matches overall demand and capacity for product families rather than individual items. It helps set production, inventory, workforce, and subcontracting levels.
An aggregate planning strategy that changes output or workforce levels to match demand closely. It can reduce inventory but may create hiring, layoff, overtime, or morale problems.

An aggregate planning strategy that keeps production or workforce levels steady while using inventory, backorders, or demand management to absorb demand changes. It provides stability but may increase inventory or waiting.
An aggregate planning strategy that combines options such as steady workforce, overtime, inventory, subcontracting, and demand management. Managers use it to balance cost, service, and flexibility.
A detailed plan showing what specific finished products will be made, how many, and when. It translates the aggregate plan into item-level production timing.
A quick check of whether major resources are sufficient to support the master production schedule. It helps identify big capacity problems before detailed planning.
A planning system that calculates what materials are needed, how much is needed, and when orders should be released. It uses the master schedule, bill of materials, inventory records, and lead times.
A list showing all components, parts, and quantities needed to make one finished product. It is like the recipe for a manufactured item.
The total amount of an item needed before considering current inventory or scheduled receipts. In MRP, gross requirements come from demand for the finished product or parent item.

The actual amount that must be ordered or produced after considering inventory on hand and scheduled receipts. Formula: Net Requirements = Gross Requirements − Available Inventory − Scheduled Receipts.
The planned timing for placing or starting orders so materials arrive when needed. MRP schedules releases by working backward from due dates using lead times.
An integrated software system that connects business functions such as operations, inventory, purchasing, accounting, sales, and HR. ERP helps companies share one set of data across departments.
Assigning jobs to machines, workers, or work centers and deciding the order in which work should be done. Good scheduling reduces delays, idle time, and late orders.
Rules used to decide the order in which jobs are processed. Different rules improve different goals, such as minimizing flow time, lateness, or setup complexity.
A sequencing rule that processes jobs in the order they arrive. It is simple and fair, but it may not minimize average waiting time or lateness.
A sequencing rule that processes the job with the shortest processing time first. It usually reduces average flow time and average waiting time.
A sequencing rule that processes the job with the earliest due date first. It is often used when meeting due dates is the main priority.
A scheduling rule that compares time remaining until due date with work time remaining. Formula: Critical Ratio = Time Remaining ÷ Work Remaining; values below 1 signal a job is behind schedule.
The total time required to complete a set of jobs from start to finish. Managers often try to reduce makespan to improve capacity use and delivery speed.
The total time a job spends in the system, including waiting and processing. Formula: Flow Time = Completion Time − Arrival Time.
How far a job's completion time is from its due date. Formula: Lateness = Completion Time − Due Date; negative lateness means the job finished early.
How late a job is when it finishes after its due date. Formula: Tardiness = max(0, Completion Time − Due Date).
Planning the timing of service workers, appointments, rooms, equipment, and customer flow. Service scheduling is difficult because demand is often variable and service cannot usually be stored.
A temporary effort with a specific goal, deadline, and set of resources. Projects are different from routine operations because they are unique and have a defined end point.
The process of planning, organizing, leading, and controlling work to complete a project on time, within budget, and according to scope. It balances time, cost, quality, and stakeholder needs.
The person responsible for coordinating the project team, schedule, resources, risks, and communication. A good project manager keeps work moving and removes obstacles.
Any person or group affected by the project or able to influence it. Stakeholders can include customers, employees, managers, suppliers, regulators, and sponsors.
A document that formally authorizes a project and summarizes its purpose, goals, scope, stakeholders, and high-level constraints. It gives the project manager authority to begin work.
The work and deliverables included in a project. Clear scope helps prevent uncontrolled additions called scope creep.
A hierarchical breakdown of a project into smaller deliverables and tasks. It helps managers organize work, assign responsibility, estimate cost, and build schedules.
The smallest manageable unit of work in a work breakdown structure. A work package should be specific enough to estimate, assign, schedule, and track.
A major checkpoint or event in a project, often marking completion of an important deliverable or phase. Milestones help track progress without showing every task detail.
A visual project schedule that shows activities as bars across a timeline. It helps managers see task timing, overlap, deadlines, and progress.
A project planning diagram that shows activities and their order relationships. It helps managers find dependencies and the critical path.
A network diagram format where each activity is shown as a node or box, and arrows show precedence relationships. It is commonly used for CPM and PERT analysis.
A dependency showing which project activities must happen before others can start or finish. Precedence relationships determine the logical order of project work.
A project scheduling method used to identify the longest path of dependent activities. It shows the shortest possible project duration and which tasks cannot be delayed.
The longest chain of dependent activities in a project network. Any delay on the critical path delays the entire project unless time is recovered elsewhere.
The total time required to complete a project. In CPM, project duration is determined by the length of the critical path.
The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the overall project. Formula: Slack = Latest Start − Earliest Start, or Latest Finish − Earliest Finish.
Another term for slack time, meaning how much schedule flexibility an activity has. Activities on the critical path usually have zero float.
A project scheduling method that uses optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic time estimates to handle uncertainty. It is useful when activity times are not known with confidence.
The shortest reasonable time an activity could take if things go very well. In PERT, it is one of three estimates used to calculate expected activity time.
The longest reasonable time an activity could take if major problems occur. In PERT, it helps account for uncertainty and downside risk.
The best estimate of how long an activity will usually take under normal conditions. In PERT, it receives the most weight in the expected time formula.
The weighted average activity time used in PERT. Formula: Expected Time = (Optimistic + 4 × Most Likely + Pessimistic) ÷ 6.
Adjusting a project schedule to reduce resource overloads or uneven use of people, equipment, or money. It may make the schedule more realistic but can extend the project.
Shortening a project schedule by adding resources or spending more money on critical path activities. It is used when finishing earlier is worth the extra cost.
The decision about whether reducing project time is worth the additional cost. Managers compare the cost of speeding up work with the value of finishing earlier.
The process of identifying, analyzing, and responding to risks that could affect project scope, schedule, cost, or quality. Good risk planning reduces surprises.
A flexible project approach that delivers work in short cycles and adapts based on feedback. It is useful when requirements are uncertain or likely to change.
A short, fixed period of work in agile project management, often one to four weeks. The team aims to complete a usable piece of work by the end of the sprint.
An agile framework where teams organize work into sprints, hold regular check-ins, and continuously improve. Common roles include product owner, scrum master, and development team.
The formal ending of a project after deliverables are completed, accepted, or stopped. Closure includes final documentation, handoff, payment, evaluation, and release of resources.
A review of what went well, what went wrong, and what should be improved in future projects. Capturing lessons learned helps organizations avoid repeating mistakes.
